title: Australia GP Winners and Losers: Who left Melbourne up, and who left with questions
meta: Published: 9 March 2026 | By Lena Müller, Strategy Analyst | F1CC News
caption: Eetu Väisänen after winning the Australian GP.
body:
Let’s rewind to lap 7, turn 3. Julian Kondra’s Alpine is beached across the fast chicane, the safety car blinking into life. Every car on the track dives for the pit lane. That single decision window — pit now, or stay out — branches the race into three distinct futures. Melbourne was not one race. It was a decision tree, and the branches punished those who took the wrong path.

The strategic pattern was clear. An early full‑wet reset after Kondra’s crash. A mid‑race crossover window where the track surface hovered between wet and intermediate — the phase where races are won or lost. And a late puncture cascade that struck anyone still running worn, overheated wets when the rain returned with a vengeance. The final order diverged sharply from the running order because some teams covered the phases, and others were exposed.

The decisive sequence: After the safety‑car restart, most of the field committed to full wets. When the rain slackened, Mercedes, Alpine, and Williams took the bold offset — intermediates, right in the crossover window. At first, that call looked traffic‑limited. But once the full wets began overheating, the intermediate runners gained a free pit‑stop phase. As heavier rain reloaded the circuit, teams had to cycle back to fresh wets — and those who had stretched their first wet set through the drying phase were the ones most exposed when water load rose. George Konstantaras triggered the first major puncture alarm. Then the failures spread through the pack, consuming leaders who had gambled on tyre life lasting just one lap longer.

That phase‑by‑phase coverage explains why the final order looks so different from the earlier running.

Winners

Eetu Väisänen (Ferrari, P1 from P1): Väisänen covered every window. He controlled the wet opening, survived the early safety‑car shuffle that dropped him from first to fourth, and then stayed out of the reactive chaos that consumed the front. While others were forced into emergency stops and puncture risk, Väisänen’s pit wall hit every offset perfectly. He pitted on lap 31 — the moment the deluge arrived — and inherited a lead that never left him. The alternative? Wait one lap, and he would have been among the puncture victims. The cost of that alternative would have been 20 points lost. He got the decision right.

Wesley Vandesteene (Mercedes, P2 from P5): Vandesteene’s race is a textbook recovery through decision‑tree coverage. He initially lost time behind his teammate in a double‑stack under safety car — an early branch that put him on the back foot. But Mercedes then read the crossover window correctly, switching Vandesteene to intermediates while the track still favoured wets. The “gamble is working” phase delivered him a net gain of track position, and he converted that advantage into a podium. From fifth on the grid, he turned what could have been a day of damage limitation into a second‑place finish. What if Mercedes had stacked him behind Zwart for the late‑race wet stop, too? He would have fallen behind the puncture cascade. The offset they gave him in the intermediate phase bought just enough air.

Michiii MaraDöner (Alpine, P3 from P8): MaraDöner’s rise came entirely during that same crossover window. The commentary feed lit up with his overtakes once the inter pace came alive, and by the time the field reset on fresh wets, he had converted a speculative strategy call into podium track position. P8 to P3 is not a holding race; it’s an attack race, and it only works if the pit wall gives you the tyre phase that matches your aggression. Alpine covered the inter window, and MaraDöner made the numbers count.

Sarah Springfield (Aston Martin, P6 from P18): Springfield’s value came from surviving the puncture phase. She was one of the few not named in the failure wave, and that stability — choosing the right moment to switch off the worn wets before they gave up — converted a deep grid slot into six points. She covered the return‑to‑wets window before it became an emergency, and that is a strategy decision, not luck.

Puncture survivors in the points: Springfield (P18 to P6), Zhao Lei (P13 to P7), Riley Swansson (P16 to P9), and Lukus Wright (P12 to P10) all emerged from the puncture chaos with points because they, or their pit walls, read the collapse of the worn‑wet phase early enough. In this race, tyre preservation under uncertainty was a strategic result, not merely a driving one.

Mercedes as a unit: Despite an early double‑stack that cost Vandesteene track position, Mercedes left Melbourne with P2 and P5 from P5 and P3 on the grid. The team read the crossover window better than any of their direct rivals and recovered from the initial disruption to bank the strongest two‑car score. The decision tree they followed — early inter offset, then conservative return to wets — covered both drivers through the puncture phase.

Losers

Afonso Cachinho (Ferrari, P12 from P16): Cachinho’s day was a series of branch points taken reactively. He qualified P16, needed a clean attrition climb to reach the points, and instead got caught in the puncture wave. Commentary flagged him early as under pressure, and the race never gave him a window to recover. The result leaves Ferrari’s scoreboard lopsided: a race win on one side, zero points on the other. The gap between the two scarlet cockpits was not just pace — it was phase‑coverage. Cachinho was on the wrong branch at the wrong time.

Red Bull conversion (both cars out of points): Verhaeren qualified P7 but started from the pit lane — a pre‑race branch that made the rest of his afternoon an exercise in salvage. He recovered to P11, which is a strong drive in isolation, but the team‑level outcome remains a loser because neither Red Bull scored after showing pace that could have targeted the top ten. Jason Roy, running third before the lap‑31 puncture cascade, was one of the primary victims of the worn‑wet failure wave. Red Bull entered the race with two cars in the top seven on pure pace; they leave Melbourne with zero points. What if they had pitted Roy one lap earlier, before the rain peak? He would have cleared the puncture window and likely finished on the podium. One lap. That’s the cost of reading the phase late.

Robin Keisner and Audi’s race management (P15 from P10): Keisner started inside the top ten and was repeatedly caught in traffic or incident‑adjacent moments through the stop phases. This was a conversion miss, not a pace discovery weekend. Audi’s pit wall had the early safety‑car mis‑step, and then failed to offset Keisner into the inter window that Mercedes and Alpine used. The result is a finish three places lower than his starting position — a loss that haunts the points‑scoring expectation.

Joshua Nathanial (McLaren, DNF from P9): Nathanial’s retirement at the same low‑grip, curb‑sensitive corner that claimed Kondra was a branch that cut McLaren’s strategy options in half. Once he was out, the team could only cover the phases with one car. Zhao Lei salvaged points, but the two‑car strategy flexibility that defines modern race management was gone.

The puncture victims: The failure wave started with Konstantaras (started P20) as the trigger, then consumed Roy (P7 start), Cachinho (P16 start), and others. The pattern was identical: worn wet tyres, overheated during the drier crossover phase, then loaded with heavy rain again. The decision to stretch the first wet set through the drying window carried a hidden cost: degraded compound integrity that collapsed as soon as water load rose. Teams that covered the return‑to‑wets window early avoided the punctures. Those that gambled on one more lap paid with their races.

The teams that missed the timing: This race did not punish lack of pace as much as it punished late reactions. The teams that read the crossover window and the return‑to‑wets window cleanly rose; those that stayed out one phase too long paid with punctures, emergency stops, and lost races. The operating rule for 2026 is already defined: in unstable weather, strategy timing is performance. Now, here’s the haunting question: what if you had pitted one lap earlier? For Red Bull, that’s the difference between a double‑points finish and a zero. For Mercedes, it’s the difference between a double‑stack cost and a possible victory. The branches are set. Melbourne won’t decide the title, but it has already taught us which pit walls read the tree best.
